mnemonics.title: ARM Modes Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: Remember ARM privilege modes with: 'U S I F A U S' - User, Supervisor, IRQ, FIQ, Abort, Undefined, System. A helpful phrase: 'Uncle Sam's Infantry For Africa Under Siege'.

mnemonics.title: CPSR Flags
mnemonics.content: NZCV flags: Negative, Zero, Carry, oVerflow. Mnemonic: 'Not Zero Carry Violated' to remember flag meanings.

mnemonics.title: Pipeline Stages ARM7
mnemonics.content: FDE: Fetch, Decode, Execute - 'Famous Delicious Eat' to remember 3-stage pipeline.

acronyms.expansion: Advanced RISC Machines / Acorn RISC Machine
acronyms.usage: Processor architecture for embedded and mobile devices
Design philosophy with simple, few instructions
Holds processor flags and mode information
Stores return address for subroutine calls
Holds address of next instruction to execute
Points to top of stack in memory
Transfer data from memory to register
Transfer data from register to memory

visualTips.visualization: Picture two separate areas: a register bank (small boxes) and memory (large storage). Only LDR/STR bridge between them; ALU operations stay within register area.
Imagine an assembly line: one instruction being fetched while another is being decoded, and a third is executing - all happening simultaneously in different pipeline stages.
Think of each ARM instruction as having a 'traffic light' - Green (execute) or Red (skip) based on flags in CPSR.
Picture separate sets of registers that instantly 'appear' when entering FIQ mode - no need to save/restore the general purpose registers.

analogies.analogy: Like a library system: you can't read books in the stacks (memory). You must borrow (LDR) to your desk (register), read/analyze (ALU operations), then return (STR) the book.
Like laundry: while one load is drying, another is being washed, and a third is being folded - multiple tasks in different stages simultaneously.
Like designing a car for fuel efficiency: simpler engine (fewer transistors), no unnecessary features, efficient operation - perfect for long battery life.
Like shorthand writing: 16-bit Thumb is like abbreviations (compact but needs context), 32-bit ARM is like full sentences (clear but longer).
